Package coxprogramming.processing.sprites

Class AnimatedSprite

Object
Sprite
CompoundSprite
AnimatedSprite
public class AnimatedSprite extends CompoundSprite
A sprite that creates animation by showing one sprite frame at a time.

An animation is really a fast series of pictures. In SpriteGame, each picture can be a regular Sprite. For example, a walking character might have one sprite for "left foot forward" and another sprite for "right foot forward." AnimatedSprite stores those sprites as frames and draws only the current frame.

AnimatedSprite extends CompoundSprite. That means it uses the same group movement idea: when the animated sprite moves, all of its frame sprites move with it and stay lined up. The difference is that a CompoundSprite draws every child sprite, while an AnimatedSprite draws only one child sprite at a time.

frameNumber is the index of the frame being shown right now. It starts at 0, so the first frame is frame 0, the second frame is frame 1, and so on.

frameRate is a ratio: game frames per animation frame. If frameRate is 8, then each animation picture stays on the screen for 8 calls to drawSprite() before the next picture appears. Smaller numbers animate faster. Larger numbers animate more slowly.

Typical use:

 AnimatedSprite cloud = AnimatedSprite.FromSprite(frame0)
     .AddFrameSprite(frame1)
     .AddFrameSprite(frame2)
     .setFrameRate(10);

 cloud.setVelocity(1, 0);

 // In draw():
 cloud.move();
 cloud.drawSprite();

    Creates an empty AnimatedSprite at position (x, y) with a starting group velocity.

  • Field Details

    • frameNumber

      protected int frameNumber
      Index of the frame currently being drawn. The first frame is 0, not 1.

    • frameRate

      protected int frameRate
      Game frames per animation frame. A value of 8 means "hold each picture for 8 game frames."

    • frameCounter

      protected int frameCounter
      Counts how many game frames have passed since the animation last changed to a new frame. Students usually do not need to change this directly.

  • Constructor Details

    • AnimatedSprite

      public AnimatedSprite(PApplet parent, float x, float y)
      Creates an empty AnimatedSprite at position (x, y). You can add frames later with AddFrameSprite(Sprite).
      Parameters:
      parent - your Processing sketch; pass this
      x - the starting x position of the animation anchor
      y - the starting y position of the animation anchor

    • AnimatedSprite

      public AnimatedSprite(PApplet parent, float x, float y, float dx, float dy)
      Creates an empty AnimatedSprite at position (x, y) with a starting group velocity. Every frame sprite added later will receive this group velocity, so all frames move together.
      Parameters:
      parent - your Processing sketch; pass this
      x - the starting x position of the animation anchor
      y - the starting y position of the animation anchor
      dx - horizontal group velocity in pixels per frame
      dy - vertical group velocity in pixels per frame

  • Method Details

    • FromSprite

      public static AnimatedSprite FromSprite(Sprite s)
      Starts an AnimatedSprite from a first frame sprite.

      This is the easiest way to build an animation. The new AnimatedSprite uses the first frame's position as its own anchor point. Add more frames by chaining AddFrameSprite(Sprite).

      Parameters:
      s - the first sprite frame
      Returns:
      a new AnimatedSprite containing s as frame 0

    • fromSprite

      public static AnimatedSprite fromSprite(Sprite s)
      Standard Java-style alias for FromSprite(Sprite). It does exactly the same thing, but starts with a lowercase letter.
      Parameters:
      s - the first sprite frame
      Returns:
      a new AnimatedSprite containing s as frame 0

    • AddFrameSprite

      public AnimatedSprite AddFrameSprite(Sprite s)
      Adds a sprite as the next animation frame and returns this.

      Frame sprites use normal screen positions, just like sprites inside a CompoundSprite. Place each frame where it should appear before adding it. Most animations use frames that start at the same position, so switching frames does not make the picture jump.

      The method returns this same AnimatedSprite, so calls can be chained together in one readable setup statement.

      Parameters:
      s - the sprite frame to add
      Returns:
      this AnimatedSprite, for chaining

    • addFrameSprite

      public AnimatedSprite addFrameSprite(Sprite s)
      Standard Java-style alias for AddFrameSprite(Sprite). It does exactly the same thing, but starts with a lowercase letter.
      Parameters:
      s - the sprite frame to add
      Returns:
      this AnimatedSprite, for chaining

    • setFrameRate

      public AnimatedSprite setFrameRate(int frameRate)
      Sets how many game frames each animation frame stays visible.

      Example: if your sketch draws 60 game frames per second and this value is 10, the animation changes pictures 6 times per second. Values less than 1 are treated as 1 because an animation frame must stay visible for at least one game frame.

      Parameters:
      frameRate - game frames per animation frame
      Returns:
      this AnimatedSprite, for chaining

    • getFrameRate

      public int getFrameRate()
      Returns how many game frames each animation frame stays visible. This is the same value set by setFrameRate(int).
      Returns:
      game frames per animation frame

    • setFrameNumber

      public AnimatedSprite setFrameNumber(int frameNumber)
      Sets the current animation frame.

      The value wraps into the valid range. For example, if there are 4 frames, setting frame 5 will show frame 1. This keeps the animation from crashing when a number is too large or too small.

      Parameters:
      frameNumber - the new frame index, starting at 0
      Returns:
      this AnimatedSprite, for chaining

    • getFrameNumber

      public int getFrameNumber()
      Returns the current animation frame index, starting at 0. This is useful for debugging or for showing animation state on screen.
      Returns:
      current frame index

    • getFrameSprites

      public ArrayList<Sprite> getFrameSprites()
      Returns the sprites used as animation frames. The first sprite in the list is frame 0.
      Returns:
      the frame sprite list

    • getFrameCount

      public int getFrameCount()
      Returns the number of sprites in this animation. This is the total number of animation frames.
      Returns:
      frame count

    • nextFrame

      public AnimatedSprite nextFrame()
      Manually advances to the next animation frame.

      Most sketches do not need this because drawSprite() advances the animation automatically. Use this method when you want button presses, mouse clicks, or other game events to control the frame yourself.

      Returns:
      this AnimatedSprite, for chaining

    • previousFrame

      public AnimatedSprite previousFrame()
      Manually moves to the previous animation frame. Like nextFrame(), this is useful when game events should control the animation frame directly.
      Returns:
      this AnimatedSprite, for chaining

    • drawSprite

      public void drawSprite()
      Draws the current frame sprite, then advances the animation clock.

      Call this once per game frame inside Processing's draw() function. If no frame sprites have been added yet, this method simply returns without drawing anything.

      Overrides:
      drawSprite in class CompoundSprite

    • advanceAnimationClock

      protected void advanceAnimationClock()
      Updates the current frame after enough game frames have passed. This helper is protected because it is part of how the class works internally, not something most students need to call.
